title: Rigid heddle block weave sampler
subtitle: How to use a pick-up stick on a rigid heddle loom to weave a two-colour block pattern
excerpt: Block weave on a rigid heddle uses a pick-up stick to lift additional warp threads beyond the basic plain weave, creating two distinct blocks of colour or texture across the width. This sampler introduces the three-shed sequence needed for block weave and is suitable for weavers who have completed a basic plain weave project.
sourceNotes: Block weave on a rigid heddle is documented in Ashford and Schacht instruction materials and in UK weaving guild publications. The three-shed pick-up sequence described is observation-led from guild teaching.

glossaryTerms.definition: A weave structure in which groups of warp threads are raised or lowered in separate blocks, creating areas of contrasting colour or texture across the cloth. On a rigid heddle, blocks are created using a pick-up stick to lift additional slotted warp threads.

A flat, pointed stick used to manually lift specific warp threads, enabling weave structures beyond plain weave on a rigid heddle or simple frame loom. The stick is placed on edge to open a new shed, then laid flat to close it.

The number of warp ends per centimetre (or per inch), determining how closely packed the warp threads are. The sett controls whether the finished cloth is warp-dominant, weft-dominant, or balanced, and must be matched to the yarn weight.

content.text: Standard rigid heddle weaving produces plain weave: the simplest over-under interlacement.
expands on this by adding a third shed using a

loaded during warping. This third shed lifts a different set of slotted threads, creating a block of pattern weft floating on the surface while the remaining warp weaves in plain weave. The result is a cloth with two distinct zones: a block of pattern floats and a block of plain weave, reversing with each sequence.

items.name: DK or worsted weight yarn, colour A for warp and tabby weft
DK or worsted weight yarn, colour B for pattern weft
Rigid heddle loom, 10-dent reed, minimum 30 cm weaving width
Pick-up stick, at least as long as the heddle is wide

content.text: (10 ends per 10 cm for DK weight on a 10-dent reed), load the pick-up stick into the warp from the front of the heddle before you begin weaving. With the heddle in the neutral position, go under every alternate slotted thread across the full width and load it onto the pick-up stick. When the pick-up stick is on edge behind the heddle, it lifts those threads into a new shed; when laid flat, those threads drop back to neutral. You now have three distinct sheds: heddle up, heddle down, and pick-up stick on edge.

The standard block weave sequence is: (1) heddle up, pass colour A tabby weft; (2) pick-up stick on edge, pass colour B pattern weft; (3) heddle down, pass colour A tabby weft; (4) neutral with pick-up stick flat, pass colour B pattern weft. Repeat this four-row sequence for the length of the sampler. The pattern weft floats appear on the surface in one block and sink below the surface in the adjacent block, and the two blocks swap position with each repeat. Beat each row firmly and consistently with the heddle.

Remove the sampler from the loom and tie the warp ends in groups of four using an overhand knot to hold the weave structure at each end. Trim the fringe to an even length or fold and stitch it to the back for a cleaner edge. Hand wash the sampler in warm water and press under a damp cloth while still slightly damp to set the weave.

items.problem: The blocks are not clearly defined and the pattern weft sinks where it should float
items.cause: The pick-up stick was not set to include every alternate slotted thread consistently across the full width
items.fix: Re-load the pick-up stick from scratch, checking each slotted thread individually. The pick-up pattern must be perfectly regular for the blocks to alternate correctly.

items.problem: The edges draw inward with each pass of the pattern weft
items.cause: The pattern weft is pulled too tight at the selvedge before beating
items.fix: Leave a gentle arc in the pattern weft before pressing it down with the heddle. Pattern floats are longer than tabby passes and need slightly more length to avoid drawing the edges in.
